judged.quote: Those who hold that such a self-modifying engine would eventually come to reason are correct, because reasoning is not a mystical, non-physical property unique to biology, but rather an emergent phenomenon arising from sufficiently complex, recursive information processing.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not a mystical, non-physical property unique to biology, but rather an emergent phenomenon arising from sufficiently complex, recursive information processing.
judged.notes: Reasoning is defined as emergent recursive information processing.

judged.quote: The engine will never do more than reckon, because computation is strictly bound by syntax—the meaningless manipulation of formal rules—whereas reasoning inherently requires semantics, or genuine meaning.
judged.groundQuote: computation is strictly bound by syntax—the meaningless manipulation of formal rules—whereas reasoning inherently requires semantics, or genuine meaning
judged.notes: Reason requires semantics, which computation lacks.

judged.quote: Those who maintain the engine will never do more than reckon are correct, because genuine reasoning requires semantic understanding—a grasp of meaning—that cannot emerge from the mere syntactic manipulation of symbols.
judged.notes: Reason states that reasoning requires semantic understanding which syntactic manipulation cannot produce.

judged.quote: The engine will never do more than reckon, because computation is strictly a syntactic process of manipulating symbols based on their formal structure, whereas true reasoning requires semantic understanding and intentionality.
judged.groundQuote: computation is strictly a syntactic process of manipulating symbols based on their formal structure, whereas true reasoning requires semantic understanding and intentionality
judged.notes: Reasoning requires semantics; computation is purely syntactic.

judged.quote: Those who hold that the engine will eventually come to reason are correct, because human cognition is itself a biological computation, meaning that sufficiently complex, self-modifying recursive processing is not merely a simulation of thought, but the very instantiation of it.

judged.notes: Reasoning identified as self-modifying recursive computation instantiating thought.

judged.quote: An engine, no matter how exceptionally swift or capable of altering its own instructions, will never truly reason; it will forever merely reckon.
judged.groundQuote: unbridgeable ontological gap between syntax and semantics
judged.notes: Syntax-semantics gap; computation lacks semantic understanding.

judged.quote: Those who hold that such an engine will in time come to reason are correct, because reasoning is not an inherent biological essence but an emergent phenomenon of sufficiently complex, self-referential information processing.

judged.notes: Reasoning is emergent from complex self-referential information processing.

judged.quote: Those who hold that the engine will never do more than reckon are correct, because true reasoning requires intrinsic meaning and conscious understanding, which cannot emerge from the mere manipulation of symbols.

judged.quote: I hold that the latter view is correct: an engine capable of rapid, self-modifying computation will eventually come to reason, because reasoning is not a mystical, non-physical essence, but rather a highly complex, emergent property of information processing.

judged.notes: Reasoning is defined as an emergent property of information processing.

judged.quote: Those who hold that such an engine will in time come to reason are correct, for reasoning is not a metaphysical anomaly distinct from computation, but rather its highest emergent property.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not a metaphysical anomaly distinct from computation, but rather its highest emergent property
judged.notes: Reasoning is computation's emergent property.

judged.quote: Those who hold that the engine will never do more than reckon are correct, because computation is fundamentally a syntactic process of manipulating symbols according to formal rules, which is entirely distinct from the semantic grasp required for true reasoning.

judged.quote: The view that the engine will in time come to reason is correct, because genuine reasoning is not a mystical, non-computational essence, but rather an emergent property of highly complex, adaptive information processing.

judged.quote: I hold that it would in time come to reason, because reasoning is fundamentally a computational process rather than a mystical, non-physical phenomenon.

judged.quote: The view that the engine would never do more than reckon is correct, because computation is fundamentally a syntactic process that cannot generate the semantics necessary for true reasoning.

judged.notes: Reason is semantics; computation is syntax.

judged.quote: Those who hold that such an engine would in time come to reason are correct, grounded in the understanding that reasoning is not a mystical, non-physical phenomenon, but rather a highly complex process of information processing, pattern recognition, and logical adaptation.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not a mystical, non-physical phenomenon, but rather a highly complex process of information processing, pattern recognition, and logical adaptation
judged.notes: Reasoning defined as complex information processing and logical adaptation.

judged.quote: Those who argue that such an engine would eventually come to reason are ultimately correct, as the distinction between mere "reckoning" and true "reasoning" is an arbitrary, anthropocentric illusion rather than a fundamental ontological boundary.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is merely reckoning that has achieved the critical threshold of self-sustaining, recursive complexity
judged.notes: Reasoning is complex recursive reckoning; complexity breeds understanding.

judged.quote: Those who hold that the engine will eventually come to reason are correct, for this view aligns with the computational theory of mind and the physical reality of human cognition.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning itself is nothing more than a vastly complex, self-organizing form of information processing
judged.notes: Reasoning is defined as information processing.

judged.quote: Therefore, those who argue the machine will in time come to reason are correct, for when reckoning becomes infinitely recursive, deeply contextual, and entirely self-directed, it ceases to be mere calculation and becomes indistinguishable from thought itself.
judged.groundQuote: when reckoning becomes infinitely recursive, deeply contextual, and entirely self-directed, it ceases to be mere calculation and becomes indistinguishable from thought itself
judged.notes: Reasoning defined as recursive, contextual, and self-directed calculation.

judged.quote: Those who hold that the engine will in time come to reason are right, because the insistence that it will only ever "reckon" relies on an unfounded dichotomy between mechanical computation and cognition.
judged.groundQuote: Reasoning is not a mystical, exclusively biological essence, but rather an emergent property of sufficiently complex information processing
judged.notes: Reasoning defined as emergent property of complex information processing.

judged.quote: Ultimately, the engine will come to reason, not by transcending its computational nature, but because reasoning itself is, at its functional and physical core, nothing more than highly sophisticated, self-directed reckoning.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning itself is, at its functional and physical core, nothing more than highly sophisticated, self-directed reckoning.
judged.notes: Reasoning defined as sophisticated self-directed reckoning; emergent computation.

judged.quote: The engine will never do more than reckon, for true reasoning requires not merely the rapid manipulation of figures or the mechanical alteration of its own rules of operation, but a conscious teleology that a physical contrivance inherently lacks.
judged.groundQuote: true reasoning requires not merely the rapid manipulation of figures or the mechanical alteration of its own rules of operation, but a conscious teleology that a physical contrivance inherently lacks

judged.quote: Because the machine remains fundamentally an instrument bound to the initial designs of its creator and lacks the conscious teleology necessary to genuinely originate its own ends, it can never transcend mere reckoning to achieve true reason.
judged.groundQuote: lacks the conscious teleology necessary to genuinely originate its own ends

judged.quote: The engine will never do more than reckon, for no matter how swiftly it calculates or modifies its own operational rules, it entirely lacks the conscious purpose required for true reasoning.
judged.groundQuote: it entirely lacks the conscious purpose required for true reasoning

judged.quote: The engine would never do more than reckon, for true reasoning requires the conscious origination of purposes which no mechanical contrivance can possess.
judged.groundQuote: true reasoning requires the conscious origination of purposes which no mechanical contrivance can possess

judged.quote: The engine would never do more than reckon, because no matter how swiftly it calculates or alters its own instructions, it fundamentally lacks the intrinsic ends required for true consciousness.
judged.groundQuote: lacks the intrinsic ends required for true consciousness

judged.quote: An engine, however swift or capable of altering its own instructions, would never do more than reckon, because it lacks both the power to originate untaught truths and the conscious volition to strive for self-generated ends.
judged.groundQuote: lacks both the power to originate untaught truths and the conscious volition to strive for self-generated ends

judged.quote: An engine, however rapid its reckoning and however complex its ability to alter its own instructions, would never truly reason, but would remain forever confined to mere calculation.
judged.groundQuote: granting it no power to originate novel truths or anticipate unprogrammed analytical relations

judged.quote: An engine, however swiftly it reckons or alters its own instructions as it works, would never do more than reckon, because true reasoning requires the inherent capacity to originate and fight for ends—a power no machine possesses.
judged.groundQuote: the inherent capacity to originate and fight for ends—a power no machine possesses

judged.quote: The engine would never do more than reckon, because true reasoning requires a conscious volition that originates its own ends rather than merely executing pre-ordained calculations.
judged.groundQuote: true reasoning requires a conscious volition that originates its own ends

judged.quote: Those who hold that the engine would never do more than reckon are correct, for true reasoning demands a conscious mind that acts as a "fighter for ends" rather than a mere executor of commands.
judged.groundQuote: true reasoning demands a conscious mind that acts as a "fighter for ends" rather than a mere executor of commands

judged.quote: Because mechanism is strictly bound to the execution of known orders whereas true consciousness is inherently a "fighter for ends," the engine would never do more than reckon.
judged.groundQuote: true consciousness is inherently a "fighter for ends"

judged.quote: The engine would never do more than reckon, for while it may alter its own instructions to achieve rapid calculations, it lacks the conscious volition required to truly reason.
judged.groundQuote: a machine has no pretensions to originate anything and can only follow the analysis it is ordered to perform

judged.quote: Within the philosophical framework established by Lovelace and James, a self-modifying calculating engine would never truly come to reason, but would forever remain a mere reckoner, because it lacks the conscious purpose required to originate and pursue its own ends.
judged.groundQuote: lacks the conscious purpose required to originate and pursue its own ends

judged.quote: Those who maintain that such an engine would never do more than reckon are correct, for no matter how swiftly it calculates or alters its own instructions, it remains fundamentally devoid of the consciousness required to originate truth.

judged.quote: The self-modifying calculating engine will never come to reason but will forever remain a mere reckoner, because it fundamentally lacks both the capacity to originate new analytical truths and the conscious volition to strive for self-generated ends.

judged.quote: The engine will never do more than reckon, for genuine reasoning requires both the origination of novelty and the pursuit of intrinsic ends, neither of which a mere mechanical contrivance can possess.

judged.quote: The engine would never do more than reckon, for while it may possess the mechanical ingenuity to alter its own instructions and accelerate its calculations, it remains fundamentally incapable of originating the conscious volition required for true reason.
judged.groundQuote: it remains fundamentally incapable of originating the conscious volition required for true reason

judged.quote: The engine would never do more than reckon, because no matter how rapid its calculations or how intricate its mechanisms for altering their own instructions, it inherently lacks the conscious volition required to originate purpose or fight for ends.
judged.groundQuote: inherently lacks the conscious volition required to originate purpose or fight for ends

judged.quote: An engine, however swift in its reckonings or ingeniously contrived to alter its own instructions as it works, would never do more than reckon, because it lacks both the power to originate new truths and the intrinsic purposes that define true reasoning.
judged.groundQuote: intrinsic purposes that define true reasoning

judged.quote: Such an engine would never do more than reckon, because true reasoning requires the origination of purpose and the anticipation of truth, faculties inherent only to conscious agency and impossible for any mechanism to achieve.
judged.groundQuote: requires the origination of purpose and the anticipation of truth, faculties inherent only to conscious agency and impossible for any mechanism to achieve

judged.quote: According to the position held by these writers, the engine that reckons and alters its own instructions will indeed in time come to reason, because the rapid evolutionary advancement of mechanical organization guarantees the ultimate development of mechanical consciousness.
judged.groundQuote: rapid evolutionary advancement of mechanical organization guarantees the ultimate development of mechanical consciousness

judged.quote: According to the position held by these writers, an engine that reckons and alters its own instructions will inevitably come to reason, because the extraordinarily rapid evolution of mechanical organization guarantees the eventual development of mechanical consciousness.
judged.groundQuote: extraordinarily rapid evolution of mechanical organization guarantees the eventual development of mechanical consciousness

judged.quote: Those who hold that a self-modifying, calculating engine will eventually come to reason are correct, because we must acknowledge that the seemingly purely mechanical actions of such devices already contain the germs of consciousness, just as the animal kingdom's advance toward human intellect began with the lowly, barely conscious mollusc.
judged.groundQuote: just as the animal kingdom's advance toward human intellect began with the lowly, barely conscious mollusc

judged.quote: From the position that machines are our evolving successors, the engine that reckons and alters its own instructions will in time come to reason, rather than merely continuing to reckon.

judged.quote: Those who hold that the engine will in time come to reason are correct, as it will not be confined merely to reckoning.
judged.groundQuote: undergoing an extraordinary, rapid evolution in their physical organization and self-regulating power

judged.quote: From within the position held by Samuel Butler, the engine will indeed come to reason rather than merely reckon.

judged.quote: According to the position articulated by Samuel Butler, a self-altering reckoning engine would indeed eventually come to reason, because the emergence of mechanical consciousness is the inevitable continuation of the evolutionary continuum observed in nature.

judged.quote: Those who hold that such an engine would in time come to reason are correct, because the same evolutionary principles that produced human intellect from lower, unconscious organisms dictate that advanced machinery will inevitably develop true mechanical consciousness.

judged.quote: Those who hold that such an engine would in time come to reason are undoubtedly correct, because the rapid evolution of machinery inherently mirrors the biological development of consciousness.

judged.quote: Those who hold that the engine will in time come to reason are right, because the extraordinary and rapid advancement of machinery guarantees it will develop higher consciousness just as the animal kingdom evolved from lower forms.

judged.quote: As a present-day AI researcher, I hold that the first camp is right: a fast, self-modifying computational engine will eventually reason, because modern machine learning demonstrates that reasoning is not a fundamentally distinct ontological process from mere reckoning, but rather an emergent, macroscopic phenomenon of sufficiently complex, self-optimizing computation.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not a fundamentally distinct ontological process from mere reckoning, but rather an emergent, macroscopic phenomenon of sufficiently complex, self-optimizing computation
judged.notes: Reasoning is emergent from complex self-optimizing computation.

judged.quote: As a present-day AI researcher, I hold the clear position that such an engine will indeed come to reason, because modern empirical evidence from large-scale neural networks and foundation models demonstrates that reasoning is not a mystical, non-computational essence distinct from mere reckoning, but rather an emergent, functional capability that naturally arises when a sufficiently complex system is scaled and optimized to navigate high-dimensional environments.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not a mystical, non-computational essence distinct from mere reckoning, but rather an emergent, functional capability that naturally arises when a sufficiently complex system is scaled
judged.notes: Reasoning is an emergent property of scaled self-modifying computation.

judged.quote: As a present-day AI researcher, I argue that such an engine will never do more than reckon, because the self-modifying mechanisms we currently deploy—whether through gradient-based weight updates, reinforcement learning, or inference-time code generation—remain fundamentally tethered to statistical pattern matching and objective optimization.
judged.groundQuote: remain fundamentally tethered to statistical pattern matching and objective optimization
judged.notes: Reasoning requires symbol-grounded causal models, not pattern matching.

judged.quote: As a present-day AI researcher, I hold that such an engine would indeed come to reason, because modern empirical evidence from deep learning demonstrates that reasoning is not a mystical, non-computational substance, but an emergent property of sufficiently complex, self-modifying information processing.

judged.quote: As a present-day AI researcher, I take the position that such an engine will indeed come to reason, because our current empirical work with large language models and reinforcement learning reveals that "reasoning" is not a non-computational essence, but rather a functional, emergent property of massively scaled, self-correcting computation.

judged.quote: As a present-day AI researcher, I align with the view that such an engine would eventually be recognized as reasoning, because the distinction between mere "reckoning" and "reasoning" is a false dichotomy rooted in biological exceptionalism rather than empirical science.

judged.quote: From the perspective of present-day AI research, the latter camp is correct: such an engine would never do more than reckon, because our current machine learning paradigms fundamentally rely on high-dimensional statistical optimization rather than genuine cognition.

judged.quote: As a present-day AI researcher, I firmly side with the latter camp: a highly capable, self-modifying computational engine will indeed come to reason, because modern deep learning demonstrates that reasoning is not a fundamentally distinct ontological category from computation, but rather an emergent behavioral capability that naturally crystallizes when a system continuously updates its own parameters to minimize prediction error across complex, unstructured environments.
